facts_disclosed: Switched to Refugio Yoga in Barrio Italia

turns.content: good morning. I am having the most perfect cup of coffee right now — fresh beans from that roaster on Calle Caupolicán, ground by hand, brewed slowly. I wanted to ask you something about stretching and muscle recovery for fieldwork
Good morning! What's going on with the muscle recovery — are you dealing with soreness from the fieldwork?

my lower back is staging a full revolt. days of crouching in courtyards and climbing scaffolding. yoga helps enormously but I want to know what I should do on non-yoga days to keep everything from locking up

Fieldwork puts your body through a lot of asymmetric loading. For non-yoga days, a few things that help specifically with crouching and climbing strain:

1. **Cat-cow stretches** — 2 minutes morning and evening for spinal mobility.
2. **Hip flexor stretches** — kneeling lunge, 30 seconds each side. Crouching tightens these badly.
3. **Supine twist** — knees to one side lying down, a minute each way to decompress the lower back.

How many days a week are you doing yoga currently?

three times a week still. I actually switched studios recently — I go to Refugio Yoga now, in Barrio Italia. much smaller place, very quiet. no fluorescent lights, just candles and these generous windows that let the afternoon light in. so much better for my head. and the space itself feels like it was designed by someone who understands that a room can either heal you or wound you

That sounds like exactly the right environment for you, especially given the migraine sensitivity. Natural light and candles instead of fluorescents would make a real difference. What style of yoga do they teach there?

hatha and some restorative classes. the teacher Francisca has this very body-aware approach — she asks about injuries, about stress, about where you are holding tension. then she adjusts the practice for you. it is not a performance, it is a conversation between you and your own structure. she gave me a modified sequence for hip openers and lower back release that I do after field days

A teacher who tailors the practice to your specific work demands is incredibly valuable. If Francisca knows about the crouching and climbing, she can target exactly the tension patterns your body develops. For non-yoga days I'd add the cat-cow and hip flexor stretches to complement her sequence. A foam roller for your mid-back and IT bands would also help — do you have one?

Diego has one. civil engineers apparently also destroy their spines. I will steal his. Francisca also says my lower back is compensating for weak abdominals, which is probably true — I have the core of a poet, not an athlete

Ha — the core of a poet. She's almost certainly right though. Weak core forces the lower back to do all the stabilization, which is a recipe for pain when you're crouching all day. Even 5-10 minutes of planks and dead bugs a few times a week would make a noticeable difference.

my body is the house I live in and I have been neglecting the foundations, which as an architect I should know is a catastrophic oversight. yalla, planks and dead bugs and a stolen foam roller. a restoration project for my own structure. thank you — time for my second coffee and then the field
A restoration project for your own structure — start with the foundations. Your body will thank you on the next scaffolding day. Enjoy the second cup and steal that foam roller before Diego notices.
